options.name: Option A — Unsynchronized core, you own threading (EclipseStore-literal model, with mandatory internal race fixes)
options.contract:
GOLDEN RULE (ported verbatim from EclipseStore, resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc:30-33 and misc/pages/locking/index.adoc:9-15): "Modifying the object graph and committing the changes must happen under the same lock." The library synchronizes ITS OWN bookkeeping (OID registry, dirty set, state transitions, Lazy holders, commit pipeline) but NEVER your objects. GUARANTEED: (1) single-threaded app code is 100% safe with zero user effort — the LazyReferenceManager daemon and asyncio thread-offloaded lazy loads cannot corrupt anything (this is non-negotiable and requires the internal fixes below; EclipseStore guarantees the same: "the lazy-clearing background thread... cannot reclaim a reference still held by application code", concurrent-access.adoc:109-111); (2) commit() is serialized by an internal mutex and atomic-on-disk (one SQLite txn, all-or-nothing — same wording as their transactions.adoc: "atomic all or nothing", durability-atomic, NOT isolation-in-RAM); (3) a write racing commit is never lost — it lands in the next commit (see mechanics, fix b); (4) library internals never corrupt, never crash, regardless of user locking. UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR (bounded): mutating reachable objects from another thread while commit() serializes, without holding the same lock — the persisted snapshot may interleave per-object states that never coexisted in memory (cross-object invariant tearing). UB is bounded to torn persisted snapshots: no internal corruption, no lost tracked writes, best-effort ConcurrentMutationError tripwire (advisory, like Java's CME). Also UB: reading the graph concurrently with mutation without a read lock (partial reads — their doc's "Partial reads" failure mode, lines 50-54). API SKETCH:
```python
db = pyrsistance.open("app.db")   # internal commit mutex only; lock file = process-level, unrelated (their note, line 132-136)
# multi-threaded? you lock. Helpers (analog of LockedExecutor/LockScope/@Read/@Write):
from pyrsistance.concurrent import RWLock, LockScope, read, write
class Customers(LockScope):                 # _rwlock is transient (never persisted)
    def add(self, c):
        with self.write():                  # mutate AND commit in one scope
            self.items.append(c); db.commit()
    def names(self):
        with self.read(): return [c.name for c in self.items]  # return copies, never live lists
@write(mutex="customers")                   # decorator analog of Spring @Write/@Mutex (LockAspect.java: global RRWLock default + named-lock ConcurrentHashMap)
def register(c): ...
# asyncio (FastAPI/strawberry): aiorwlock-style, no awaits inside write scope except the commit
async with db.alock.write():
    root.orders.append(o); await db.acommit()
```
options.mechanics: CORE: no locks on the read or steady-write path; one internal commit mutex (threading.Lock) serializes commit(); one small internal state-transition lock (striped if profiling demands) guards the tri-state machine; registry/dirty-set/Lazy-holder operations each guarded by their own internal locks (free under GIL, required under 3.14t free-threading). FIX (b) hook-flip lost update — "re-arm before scan" ordering, borrowed from concurrent-GC card tables (clean the card BEFORE scanning it): commit (1) atomically swaps the global dirty set for a fresh empty one under the transition lock; (2) for each dirty object: re-install the first-write hook and set state=Clean FIRST (one critical section under the transition lock), THEN msgspec-serialize it. Any write racing serialization re-fires the hook and lands the object in the NEW dirty set → persisted next commit. Converts "permanently untracked write" into "persisted one commit late, possibly torn under documented UB" — lost-update race eliminated without any user-facing model. Both hook-fire (Dirty+enqueue+disarm) and commit-flip (Clean+rearm) are single critical sections under the same lock, so no torn transitions even free-threaded. FIX (c) daemon demotion — adopt EclipseStore's design literally: THE DAEMON NEVER MUTATES LIVE OBJECTS. No Clean→Ghost class-swap on objects that may be referenced. Instead, demotion = reference dropping: the library's only strong refs are (i) each Lazy[T] holder's cached _ref and (ii) a keep-alive LRU of recently-touched Clean objects (this LRU is what keeps Clean objects resident at all; WeakValueDictionary itself keeps nothing alive). Under timeout/RSS pressure the daemon nulls Lazy._ref (single attribute store under the holder's micro-lock; their Lazy.clear() semantics, clearing-lazy-references.adoc:5-14) and evicts LRU entries, then gc.collect(); unreachable objects die, app-held objects stay alive untouched — use-after-clear is impossible by construction, exactly the Swizzle-Registry-is-weak argument. Class-swap ghosts survive ONLY as the load-side representation: a never-materialized OID is published as a Ghost whose __getattribute__ funnels through a per-object load lock; promotion fills slots first, swaps __class__ to the real class last (initialize-then-publish), so racing readers either block on the load lock or see a fully-built object. Promotion (Ghost→Clean) is one-way at runtime; the dangerous reverse direction no longer exists. FIX (a) tripwire (advisory only): a global write-epoch counter bumped by every first-write hook; commit records it before/after serialization; if it moved, log/raise ConcurrentMutationError in strict mode — best-effort detection à la ConcurrentModificationException (their failure mode 3, GigaMap store detecting inconsistent traversal, concurrent-access.adoc:63-73), plus msgspec's natural "dict changed size during iteration" surfaced with a helpful message. HELPERS: pure-Python RWLock (condition-variable based; stdlib has none), LockScope mixin / LockedExecutor, @read/@write/@mutex decorators (port of integrations/spring-boot3/.../concurrent/LockAspect.java: global RW lock by default, named locks partition), asyncio RWLock + FastAPI dependency + strawberry SchemaExtension wrapping queries in read / mutations in write scope. Lock attributes are transient fields excluded from persistence (EclipseStore marks LockedExecutor `transient` — locking/index.adoc:50). Per-thread Storer rule kept: the buffer-until-commit storer is confined to the committing thread (their Serializer/Storer "not thread-safe, one per thread" rule, serializer/performance.adoc:34-35, concurrent-access.adoc:117-125).
options.implementation_cost: ~2.5-3 weeks solo, mostly orthogonal pieces: flip-before-serialize commit ordering + transition lock: 1-2 days. LazyReferenceManager redesign to reference-drop eviction (keep-alive LRU + Lazy._ref nulling + gc.collect under pressure): 3-5 days — this is the biggest item because it changes how Clean objects are kept resident at all. Ghost promotion load-lock + initialize-then-publish: 1-2 days (mostly already implied by class-swap loading). Epoch tripwire + ConcurrentMutationError: 1 day. Sync helpers (RWLock, LockScope, LockedExecutor, decorators): 2-3 days. Async helpers (aio RWLock, FastAPI dependency, strawberry extension): 2-3 days. Threaded stress-test suite using EclipseStore's own recommended pattern (N writer/reader threads, tens of seconds, assert invariants in memory, then RESTART and re-assert on persisted state — concurrent-access.adoc:263-274): 2-3 days. Contract documentation page (port of concurrent-access.adoc — this artifact is load-bearing, EclipseStore's most-cited page): 1-2 days. Adds to v0.x: ~600-900 LOC library code + a docs page; no new dependencies (aiorwlock is ~150 LOC to vendor). Nothing here is throwaway — every internal fix is required by ANY future concurrency model.
options.steady_state_overhead: Reads: ZERO added cost — plain slots access (~20-30ns in CPython 3.14) with no lock, no version check. This is the entire performance argument: a per-read lock would cost 50-100ns (uncontended threading.Lock acquire+release), i.e. 3-5x read slowdown, and Option A avoids it. Steady-state writes after first touch: zero added (hook disarmed). First write per object per txn: one transition-lock acquire + set-add + hook disarm ≈ 150-300ns, once. Commit: +1 lock acquire and hook re-arm per dirty object (~100-150ns each) before serialization; msgspec encoding (~1-5µs/object) and SQLite fsync dominate, so commit latency change is <5% even for 10k dirty objects (~1.5ms added). Tripwire: two int reads per commit, free. Daemon: nulls refs under micro-locks it owns; no safepoints, no pauses for app threads; gc.collect() under RSS pressure is the only stop-the-world-ish cost (10-100ms on large heaps — schedule it, don't loop it). Helper RWLock: ~0.5-1µs per acquire — fine at request/operation granularity, never at attribute granularity. Identity-map and Lazy internal locks: nanoseconds, uncontended in the target workloads.
options.failure_modes: (1) The classic: user mutates in one method, commits in another, locks each separately — broken even though "everything is locked" (their pitfall list, concurrent-access.adoc:236-239); manifests as torn persisted snapshots discovered after restart, exactly microstream#453 (corrupted DB under stress) and #676 (No entity found for objectId). (2) Returning a live list from inside a read scope — caller mutates after lock release (their pitfall, line 244-246; their example comments "never return original one", JokesStorageImpl.java). (3) ConcurrentMutationError / msgspec "changed size during iteration" at commit = the Python face of microstream#394 (@Store → CME); detection is best-effort, silence does NOT mean safety. (4) asyncio-specific sharp edges: holding the SYNC RWLock across an await deadlocks or starves the loop (must use the async lock); an await inside a write scope yields to other requests mid-mutation — same bug as (1) wearing an async mask; strawberry resolvers fanning out to threads do unlocked partial reads unless the request-scope extension is installed. (5) Memory: because the daemon can no longer forcibly demote app-referenced objects, an app that pins the whole graph in a global list defeats RSS-pressure eviction entirely (EclipseStore has the identical property and documents it as correct behavior) — manifests as RSS growth, not corruption. (6) Free-threaded Python users will run racy user code 10x more often than GIL users; the contract holds but UB gets exercised. HONESTY ON 'DOCUMENT IT AWAY' IN 2026: it did not even hold in Java — despite java.util.concurrent literacy, MicroStream accumulated corruption issues and EclipseStore capitulated by shipping a Locking API in 2.1 (Dec 2024) and Spring @Read/@Write AOP. Python culture is one notch worse (a GIL-trained generation, async colorization, free-threading arriving). Verdict: viable for v0.x ONLY because the persona is single-threaded local-first apps AND the internal fixes make that case effortless — but helpers must ship day one (not as an afterthought), and the FastAPI/strawberry integration must install its locking by default (opt-out), inverting EclipseStore's opt-in mistake.
options.precedents: EclipseStore/MicroStream is the direct precedent, ~10 years in production Java. Their wording: "the application is responsible for synchronizing concurrent access — {product-name} cannot do it for you... the framework has nothing to lock around because the framework is not in the read/write path" (resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc:4,24); golden rule at lines 30-43; explicit thread-safety matrix (lines 80-130): manager I/O, channels, Lazy.get(), JCache = safe; object graph, Storer, Serializer = yours. How it worked out: users blew up anyway — microstream-one/microstream#394 (@Store ConcurrentModificationException inside CDI interceptor), #453 (corrupted database under stress test), #43 (inconsistent ObjectRegistry after store exception), #676 (StorageExceptionConsistency, missing objectId) — so the project progressively added: XThreads.executeSynchronized, then LockedExecutor/LockScope/StripeLockedExecutor in eclipse-serializer 2.1 (Dec 2024; https://microstream.one/blog/2024/12/02/eclipsestores-new-locking-api-handles-concurrency/, https://javapro.io/2024/11/29/eclipsestore-takes-control-of-concurrency/), Spring AOP @Read/@Write/@Mutex (resources/store/integrations/spring-boot3/src/main/java/org/eclipse/store/integrations/spring/boot/types/concurrent/LockAspect.java — global ReentrantReadWriteLock default, named-lock map), and an internally-locked GigaMap whose own docs admit per-method locking can't span logical operations (concurrent-access.adoc:214-230). Trajectory lesson: the unsynchronized contract survives, but only fortified with shipped helpers and one canonical doc page. Counter-precedents: ZODB chose per-connection copies + MVCC (the other door); Realm/ObjectBox chose MVCC because mobile devs can't be trusted with locks; SQLite ships SERIALIZED mode as default and makes its unsynchronized multi-thread mode opt-in — telling. The daemon fix precedent is EclipseStore's own Lazy/Swizzle design: clear() drops a reference, never guts an object (clearing-lazy-references.adoc:5-14), which is why their docs can truthfully say concurrent Lazy.get() is safe (concurrent-access.adoc:109-111).
options.integration_notes: TRI-STATE DIRTY TRACKING: keeps its exact shape; the fix is pure ordering (re-arm hook + flip Clean BEFORE serializing each object, both transitions under one internal lock). The hook remains the single interception point — preserve it cleanly, it is the load-bearing asset for every future model (see migration). LAZYREFERENCEMANAGER: redesigned from "demote live objects" to "drop library-owned references" (Lazy._ref nulling + keep-alive LRU eviction + gc.collect). The WeakValueDictionary identity map already fits this perfectly — it keeps nothing alive, so eviction = GC. Demotion-by-class-swap is DELETED from the daemon; class-swap remains only for load-side Ghosts (publish-before-promote with per-object load lock). This also fixes the asyncio case for free: thread-offloaded lazy loads contend only on the per-object load lock and internally-locked registry. ASYNCIO FACADE: internal structures are now thread-safe, so to_thread lazy loads are sound; ship db.alock (async RWLock) and document the one async-specific rule: no awaits inside a write scope except await db.acommit(). FASTAPI/STRAWBERRY: workers=1 lock file handles processes (same as EclipseStore's lock file, concurrent-access.adoc:132-136 — explicitly NOT thread concurrency); within the process, ship a strawberry SchemaExtension + FastAPI dependency that wraps queries in read scope and mutations in write scope BY DEFAULT (the Spring @Read/@Write analog, but opt-out instead of opt-in). COMMIT-DELTA/SIDECAR INDEXES: unaffected and actually vindicated — FTS5/usearch/pyroaring/Arrow consume already-serialized commit deltas behind the watermark, never the live graph, so they are immune to graph races (the GigaMap lesson: library-owned structures internally consistent, fed by single-writer commit stream). Arrow mirrors double as the sanctioned consistent-read escape hatch: analytics reads that need snapshot isolation use Arrow, not the live graph — this drains most of the read-lock pressure out of Option A. FREE-THREADED PYTHON (3.14t): the user-facing contract is unchanged (you own graph sync — and it's the only model that needs NO redesign under free-threading); internally, every structure that today leans on the GIL (WeakValueDictionary, dirty set, Lazy holders, transition machine) must take its explicit lock — build them locked now, the cost is nanoseconds under GIL and correctness later.
options.migration_paths: REACHABLE LATER (all additive): (1) EclipseStore's own trajectory — more helpers, striped locks, framework integrations — already budgeted. (2) "Strict/serialized mode": a global RW lock acquired inside every public entry point, SQLite-style opt-in (or flipped to default if the persona shifts server-ward) — pure wrapper, days of work. (3) Single-writer MVCC / snapshot commits: the first-write hook is the natural interception point to copy an object into a txn-private shadow on first touch; commit then serializes shadows instead of live state, eliminating UB (a) entirely without touching user code — Option A's internals (hook + buffer-until-commit storer + commit delta pipeline) are exactly the prerequisites, so this door stays wide open. (4) Actor/confinement mode (one owner thread + command queue) — a façade over the same core. FORECLOSED (one-way doors): (1) ZODB-style per-session identity maps/copies — but note precisely: this door was already closed by the ONE-shared-WeakValueDictionary decision, not by Option A; user code written under "one live instance per OID, `is` works" cannot migrate to per-connection copies without breaking identity semantics. Option A merely cements it. (2) Transactional/isolated READS on the live graph (true reader snapshot isolation without Arrow) — would need per-object multi-versioning in every instance; retrofitting after users depend on "reads see mutations immediately within my lock" is a semantic break, not just an engineering cost. (3) Once "bounded UB at commit" is documented and relied upon (e.g. users deliberately racing low-stakes telemetry writes), tightening to hard errors is a breaking change — keep the tripwire advisory-only until 1.0, then decide.
options.best_for: BEST: the declared persona — local-first/desktop/CLI/agent-memory apps that are logically single-threaded. After the internal fixes they get a literally-zero-overhead read path (no lock, no version check, plain slot access), an invisible daemon, and never need to learn the locking API; this segment is ~90% of v0.x users and Option A is strictly optimal for them. GOOD: modest FastAPI/strawberry apps (workers=1, read-heavy) using the default-installed request-scope locking and Arrow mirrors for analytics reads — equivalent ergonomics to EclipseStore-on-Spring with @Read/@Write. WORST: write-heavy multi-threaded servers needing read-write parallelism with cross-object consistency (the coarse RW lock serializes them; striped locks demand exactly the discipline this persona lacks); free-threaded-Python enthusiasts expecting data-race-freedom from the library (they get a correct library and racy app code); and any team that wants optimistic concurrency/conflict detection — that's an MVCC model (migration path 3), not Option A. Honest summary: Option A is the right v0.x default for THIS product precisely because its persona makes the contract cheap to honor — but it is only shippable with the (b) flip-ordering and (c) reference-drop-demotion fixes, because 'single-threaded apps are 100% safe' is the one promise 2026 Python culture will not forgive breaking, and the unfixed daemon breaks it for every user, not just the concurrent ones.

options.name: Option B — One shared live graph + global write barrier at commit (scoped-mutator variant)
options.contract:
API sketch:

```python
# WRITE PATH — the only supported way to mutate managed objects:
with db.mutate():                        # shared (barrier) side; reentrant per thread; ~0.33 µs
    order.total += line.amount           # first write per object: hook fires once (~0.17 µs),
    order.lines = order.lines + (line,)  #   marks Dirty, asserts scope active, self-removes
db.commit()                              # exclusive side: waits for all open scopes, blocks new
                                         # scopes; serializes dirty set (~0.1–1 µs/obj); flips
                                         # Dirty->Clean and RE-ARMS hooks under the barrier;
                                         # releases barrier; THEN does SQLite write + fsync
                                         # outside the barrier. commit() returning => durable.
async with db.mutate(): ...              # asyncio facade; acquires off-loop; NO awaits inside.
```

GUARANTEED: (1) every committed snapshot is scope-boundary-consistent — commit never serializes a memory state that interleaves the interior of any db.mutate() scope (fixes race (a) at scope granularity); (2) no lost updates: the Dirty->Clean flip + hook re-arm happen while the exclusive side is held, so no first-write can race the flip (fixes race (b)); (3) LazyReferenceManager demotion takes the exclusive side and uses class-swap-first ordering + per-OID load mutex, so demotion never runs inside a scope or commit and never tears a read on GIL builds (fixes race (c)); (4) plain getattr is wait-free — readers are never blocked by commit, scopes, or demotion.

UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR: attribute writes outside db.mutate() (strict mode: RuntimeError at the first-write hook; permissive mode: auto-wraps the single assignment in a degenerate scope — single-attribute writes stay safe, multi-object invariants are UB); in-place mutation of plain mutable containers (list.append never fires __setattr__ — use managed collections, tuple reassignment, or db.touch(obj) inside a scope); await or blocking I/O inside a scope (works but stalls commit for its duration); sharing one scope across threads/tasks; commit() called inside a scope (the committing thread's scope is auto-closed first — other scopes may interleave before the barrier, so 'my commit' may include other threads' completed scopes, exactly EclipseStore's documented 'persisted-graph divergence' semantics, accepted by design because commit is global).

READ SEMANTICS, stated honestly: readers see live, uncommitted memory. In this paradigm memory IS truth and there is no abort path that un-writes memory, so 'dirty read' is not an anomaly — it is the definition of reading. The real residual anomalies are: no repeatable reads, and cross-object inconsistency while another thread is mid-scope (EclipseStore's 'partial reads' failure). Readers wanting cross-object consistency must use Arrow snapshot mirrors (planned) or take a scope themselves. Commit-vs-reader consistency is NOT promised to readers — only commit-vs-mutator.
options.mechanics:
CORE FINDING (the variant analysis the orchestrator asked for): the 'piggyback a momentary read-lock acquire on the once-per-object first-write hook' design is REJECTED because it does not stop torn states. Proof: thread T first-writes A (hook fires, acquires+releases read side in ns), is now between statements of a logical multi-object update; A is Dirty so further writes are native and invisible; commit acquires the write side unopposed and serializes A-new/B-old — torn state persisted. The window is the entire remainder of T's logical transaction. The 'keep the hook armed per-write while a commit is pending' variant also fails: blocking T at its NEXT write merely freezes the torn state deterministically (writes 1..k already applied get serialized) and costs 10x on every write (measured: 8.2 ns native slot write vs 79.9 ns through an armed Python __setattr__; the commit-pending flag check itself adds only ~3 ns once you're in the hook, 82.9 ns total). Per-write interception cannot deliver atomicity because the system has no knowledge of logical transaction boundaries; atomicity requires boundaries, boundaries require scopes. CORRECT VARIANT: explicit mutation scopes holding the shared side of a process-global writer-preference RW lock.

Lock design: pure-Python RWLock (Lock + Condition + counters), writer-preference (a waiting writer blocks new readers, bounding commit latency by the longest currently-open scope), read side reentrant via a thread-local depth counter. Note the role inversion vs textbook usage: SHARED side = mutator scopes (parallel mutators allowed; they exclude each other only by app discipline), EXCLUSIVE side = commit + LazyReferenceManager demotion. Plain readers take nothing.

First-write hook under this design: keeps its tri-state jobs (dirty registration, self-removal) plus a thread-local 'scope active' assert (measured 165 ns total vs 80 ns no-op hook). Hook self-removal after first write remains SAFE because mutual exclusion lives at scope granularity, not hook granularity — the original tri-state optimization is preserved.

commit() sequence: (0) if calling thread holds a scope, close it (its writes are by definition complete; upgrade would deadlock); (1) acquire write side — waits for in-flight scopes, blocks new ones; (2) walk dirty set, msgspec-encode each object into immutable buffers (~0.1–1 µs/obj), extract index deltas; (3) flip Dirty->Clean, re-arm hooks (this is what closes race (b)); (4) release write side — mutators resume; (5) SQLite insert + fsync OUTSIDE the barrier (buffers are immutable copies); return after fsync so 'returned => durable'. Crash between (4) and (5): memory says clean, store lags one delta — same loss window as any buffered design; if unacceptable, hold a separate commit-serialization mutex (not the barrier) so commits don't reorder.

LazyReferenceManager daemon: demotes only Clean objects; acquires the WRITE side per demotion batch (excludes scopes and commit; double-checks Clean status under the lock), then per object: (1) swap __class__ to Ghost FIRST — a single STORE_ATTR, atomic under the GIL; (2) clear slot state. Reader safety on GIL builds: a plain LOAD_ATTR on a slots class executes type-resolution + member_get descriptor in C within one bytecode without releasing the GIL, so a reader sees either Clean-with-state (valid value) or the Ghost class (whose __getattribute__ takes a per-OID load mutex — also held by the daemon across ops (1)-(2) — and either returns residual state or triggers reload). No use-after-clear window remains. On free-threaded 3.14t this atomicity argument dies: in-place class-swap demotion is DISABLED and the daemon falls back to eviction-only — clear the strong reference inside Lazy[T] holders and let WeakValueDictionary+GC reclaim, which is exactly how Java EclipseStore's lazy-clearing thread is race-free: it never mutates the target entity, only the wrapper ('The lazy-clearing background thread uses WeakReference and cannot reclaim a reference still held by application code', concurrent-access.adoc:109-111).
options.implementation_cost: Solo-maintainer estimate: ~1.5–2 weeks total. Writer-preference reentrant RWLock + thread-local scope tracking + stress tests: 1–1.5 days (the benchmark prototype in this analysis is ~40 lines). db.mutate() sync + async context managers, strict/permissive modes, db.touch(): 1 day. Commit pipeline refactor (write-acquire, flip-under-lock, serialize, release, I/O outside; commit-inside-scope handling): 1–2 days on top of the existing buffer-until-commit storer. LazyReferenceManager hardening (write-side batches, per-OID load mutex, class-swap-first ordering, 3.14t eviction-only fallback, interleaving tests): 2–3 days — the hairiest part. Contract docs + EclipseStore-style CI stress tests (N writer/reader threads, invariant assertions in-memory and after restart, per concurrent-access.adoc:263-273): 2–3 days. Adds to v0.x: one lock primitive, two context managers, ~300–500 LOC + tests; zero storage-format changes; no new dependencies.
options.steady_state_overhead: All numbers measured on this machine, CPython 3.14.0 GIL build (/tmp/optb_bench.py, /Users/sh/pyrsistance/.venv): plain slot write 8.2 ns; plain slot read 7.9 ns (readers pay ZERO — unchanged); first-write hook with thread-local scope assert 165 ns (vs 80 ns for the bare hook, so the barrier adds ~85 ns once per object per transaction); pure-Python RWLock read acquire+release 332 ns ONCE PER SCOPE (not per object, not per write — this is the headline win of the scoped variant); subsequent writes to Dirty objects remain native 8.2 ns because the hook still self-removes; threading.Lock pair 36 ns (floor if the RWLock is later rewritten in C). Rejected armed-per-write variant for comparison: 80–83 ns on EVERY write, 10x the native path, and still torn. Commit pause (mutators blocked; readers unaffected) = wait-for-longest-open-scope + dirty_count x serialize cost + flip cost. Measured: raw msgspec msgpack encode 113 ns/record small struct; 100k dirty objects encoded in 11.4 ms (0.11 µs/obj); with the full pipeline at the project's stated ~1 µs/obj (OID swizzling, dirty-set walk, index-delta extraction): 1k dirty ≈ 1 ms, 10k ≈ 10 ms, 100k ≈ 100 ms, 1M ≈ 1 s. fsync (1–10 ms SSD; macOS F_FULLFSYNC can be 15–100 ms) is outside the barrier and does not pause mutators.
options.failure_modes: (1) Writes outside scopes: strict mode raises deterministically at first write (good); permissive mode silently degrades multi-object invariants to UB — a user who forgets the scope gets exactly race (a) back, manifesting as rare cross-object inconsistencies in the persisted store that never existed in memory. (2) In-place container mutation (entity.list.append(x)) fires no __setattr__: invisible to dirty tracking AND the barrier — manifests as silently missing data after restart (lost update class). Needs managed collection types or db.touch(); this is orthogonal to the barrier and remains the single biggest correctness trap. (3) Long scopes: blocking I/O, network, or (async) awaits inside db.mutate() stall every commit — manifests as commit latency spikes; writer preference means they also stall OTHER new scopes once a commit is queued (convoy). (4) Deadlock shapes: commit() while holding an unrelated app lock that an open scope wants; nested scope across threads; mitigated by reentrancy + auto-close-on-commit but app-level lock ordering is the user's problem. (5) Readers still see torn cross-object states while a scope is mid-flight — by design; users who treat live getattr as a consistent snapshot will write baffling reports (EclipseStore's 'partial reads', concurrent-access.adoc:50-54); push them to Arrow mirrors. (6) Daemon-induced blips: demotion batches take the write side; first-writes/scopes stall for the batch duration (µs-scale per object; keep batches small). (7) Free-threaded builds: class-swap demotion disabled -> RSS pressure relief is weaker (eviction-only); apps holding strong refs to large Clean subgraphs keep them resident. (8) Crash between barrier release and fsync: one committed-in-memory delta lost on restart; memory/store divergence is bounded to the last commit.
options.precedents: (1) EclipseStore itself — the canonical precedent, in-repo: /Users/sh/pyrsistance/resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc states the exact rule Option B mechanizes: 'Modifying the object graph and storing the changes must happen under the same lock' (lines 30-33), catalogs the identical failure modes (partial reads :50-54; persisted-graph divergence :56-61; store-while-mutating :63-73 = races a/b), and recommends ReentrantReadWriteLock as 'what most EclipseStore applications end up using' (:160-166). Helper classes LockedExecutor/LockScope (docs/modules/misc/pages/locking/index.adoc:44-89) and Spring AOP @Read/@Write/@Mutex (integrations/spring-boot3/.../concurrent/LockAspect.java) are production-deployed scope APIs — db.mutate() is their Pythonization. GigaMap is the strongest precedent for 'commit takes the write side': it holds its internal RW lock for the DURATION of gigaMap.store(), and storing without that lock 'can leave the GigaMap in an inconsistent state and cause the store to fail' (concurrent-access.adoc:100-107, 219-230). Outcome in the wild: works, but per-operation locks proved insufficient for multi-call invariants (:226-228) — which is precisely why Option B puts scopes, not per-write locks, in the contract. (2) Smalltalk images (Squeak/Pharo snapshot:andQuit:): stop-the-world image save, decades of desktop use — proves global pauses are acceptable in the local-first persona, though full-image saves pause for seconds where Option B's delta commit pauses for ms. (3) Clojure STM refs/dosync: full in-memory MVCC shipped in 2008; community verdict is that refs went nearly unused — apps converged on atoms + immutability (single CAS, coarse granularity). Lesson: fine-grained in-memory transactions are over-engineering for this class of system; coarse barriers win on adoption. (4) LMAX Disruptor single-writer principle: confine mutation to one thread, zero locks — the persona's happy path; Option B is the safety net costing ~330 ns/scope when that discipline holds and correctness when it doesn't. (5) Redis BGSAVE fork-COW: snapshot WITHOUT pausing mutators — the road not taken: fork is unusable here (daemon threads + SQLite connections + macOS fork-after-threads breakage). (6) LMDB/BoltDB: single writer + MVCC page snapshots for readers — shows exactly what Option B's readers give up (snapshots) and what the Arrow mirrors compensate. (7) ZODB: in-place ghosting (_p_deactivate) is safe only under strict per-Connection thread confinement — evidence that pyrsistance's unconfined shared map makes demotion the most dangerous component, justifying the write-side + ordering treatment.
options.integration_notes: Tri-state dirty tracking: fully preserved — the hook keeps firing once per object per transaction, gains only a thread-local scope assert (+85 ns), and still self-removes; exclusion moved from hook granularity to scope granularity, which is what makes self-removal sound. The Dirty->Clean re-arm executes under the write side, closing race (b) exactly. LazyReferenceManager: demotes only Clean objects, in small batches under the write side, with class-swap-first ordering + per-OID load mutex (race (c) closed on GIL builds); never demotes during scopes or commit by construction; on 3.14t switches to eviction-only (clear Lazy[T] strong refs, Java-parity model per concurrent-access.adoc:109-111) — the class-swap ghost optimization becomes a GIL-build-only fast path. Asyncio facade: `async with db.mutate()` acquires the read side via executor/non-blocking retry so the event loop never parks on the barrier; hard rule 'no await inside a scope' (an awaiting scope holds the barrier across event-loop turns and exposes torn state to sibling request handlers); lazy loads are reads and need no barrier, so thread-routed loads stay lock-free, but a lazy load INSIDE a scope holds the scope across SQLite I/O — document 'pre-touch lazies before opening the scope'. FastAPI/strawberry: a strawberry extension/middleware opens a scope around mutation resolvers only; queries run unsynchronized against live memory; commit can run per-mutation or batched on a background task (global commit means batching multiple requests' scopes into one commit is well-defined); workers=1 lock file already guarantees single process. Free-threaded 3.14t future: the barrier design carries over UNCHANGED and goes from belt-and-suspenders to load-bearing (the GIL no longer serializes hook bodies); only two GIL-dependent arguments need replacing — LOAD_ATTR atomicity for demotion (solved by eviction-only) and 'getattr is effectively atomic' for readers (3.13t/3.14t per-object locks keep individual reads memory-safe and crash-free, but app-level stale/unordered reads must be documented); pure-Python RWLock works on 3.14t as-is, with a C rewrite as a later optimization (floor ~36 ns, measured threading.Lock pair).
options.migration_paths: Reachable later, additively: (1) a third lock mode — reader-shared scopes that exclude mutator scopes but not each other (group mutual exclusion) — gives readers consistent views with `with db.read():`, no API break since scopes already exist; (2) per-object seqlocks/epochs to shrink commit pauses (serialize unscoped-quiescent objects incrementally, barrier only for stragglers); (3) Arrow snapshot read path (already planned) as the MVCC-for-analytics answer; (4) read-only frozen-copy sessions hydrated fresh from the store — does not violate the identity map if they are explicitly distinct frozen instances (answers part of concern (d) for READ workloads); (5) degenerate auto-scoping (every write wrapped) if explicit scopes prove too intrusive for the CLI persona — a config flag, not a redesign; (6) C-extension RWLock for the 36 ns floor. FORECLOSED (one-way doors): ZODB-style per-session WRITABLE copies with merge/conflict-resolution — the process-global identity map plus 'memory is truth' make `load(oid) is load(oid)` a public invariant; retrofitting writable per-session views means breaking that identity or proxying every access (total API break) — concern (d) is real and Option B walks through that door deliberately; true MVCC over live objects (versioned slots) is incompatible with plain slots-dataclasses as the canonical form; fork-based snapshot commit is foreclosed by the daemon thread + SQLite handles. Soft one-way door: once `with db.mutate():` is in user code, its semantics can be strengthened but never removed — ship permissive mode as default for single-threaded users, strict mode for the multithreaded ones.
options.best_for: Best: the actual persona — single-writer local-first/desktop/CLI/agent-memory apps where one thread (or one asyncio loop) mutates and background threads occasionally read; they pay ~330 ns per logical transaction, 8 ns per subsequent write, zero on reads, and get crash-consistent scope-atomic commits plus a deterministic error (strict mode) the day they accidentally add a second writer thread. Also good: FastAPI/strawberry workers=1 with read-heavy GraphQL and short mutation resolvers (10–50 ms commit pauses at 10k–100k dirty objects are invisible at request timescales). Worst: write-heavy genuinely multi-threaded workloads — parallel mutator scopes don't exclude each other (app must still partition data), and large commits stop all mutators (1M dirty ≈ 1 s pause, Smalltalk-image territory); analytics needing repeatable reads over live objects (must detour through Arrow mirrors); and anyone expecting database-style per-request isolation — Option B is honestly 'shared memory with a consistent save button', not a transactional store, and the contract should say so in those words.

options.name: Option C — Session-scoped views (ZODB-style per-session copies, optimistic concurrency, ambient default session)
options.contract:
GUARANTEED: (1) Snapshot isolation per session — every read inside a session sees the store as of session start (or last commit/refresh); ZODB's exact promise: "Whenever we look at ZODB data, we see its state as of the time the transaction began" (zodb.org/en/latest/guide/transactions-and-threading.html). (2) One live instance per OID *per session* (identity map is session-scoped, like ZODB's per-Connection PickleCache and SQLAlchemy's "unique copies of each object" per Session). (3) Commit is atomic and validated: per-object TID compare-and-swap; on mismatch `Conflict` (a TransientError) is raised BEFORE any byte hits disk — first committer wins. (4) Confinement: no pyrsistance machinery (including LazyReferenceManager) ever touches a session's objects from another thread. (5) Cross-session references are a defined error (`ForeignSessionError`, analogue of ZODB Connection.add's `raise InvalidObjectReference(obj, obj._p_jar)`). UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR: using one session or its objects from two concurrently running threads/tasks; mutating objects after session close (close ghosts everything, so this raises `SessionClosedError` loudly rather than going silently stale); side effects inside `@db.transactional` bodies (they re-run on conflict); relying on `a is b` across sessions. API SKETCH:
```python
db = pyrsistance.open("app.db")          # Store: SQLite pool, storage, indexes
with db.session() as s:                  # explicit session, commit-on-exit/abort-on-error
    root = s.root()                      # ghosts hydrate lazily into s's identity map
    root.todos.append(Todo("milk"))      # first-write hook -> s.dirty
@db.transactional(retries=3)             # ZODB `for attempt in tm.attempts(): with attempt:`
def add_todo(s, text): s.root().todos.append(Todo(text))
db.bind_ambient()                        # local-first mode: long-lived session in a ContextVar
root = db.root(); ...mutate...; db.commit()   # feels exactly like the shared-graph design
s2 = db.session(before=tid)              # read-only historical session (ZODB historical_pool precedent)
```
options.mechanics: CONFINEMENT + MVCC + OPTIMISTIC COMMIT, no graph locks. Each Session owns: `_imap: dict[oid, obj]` (strong refs — session lifetime bounds object lifetime; ambient session uses weakrefs+LRU), `_dirty: set[oid]`, `_read_tids: dict[oid, tid]`, and a borrowed SQLite connection holding an open WAL read transaction = a free `loadBefore` analogue (SQLite WAL snapshot reads ARE ZODB's MVCC; ZODB had to build MVCCAdapter — `self._mvcc_storage = MVCCAdapter(storage)` in DB.py — we get it from the storage engine). Objects carry hidden slots `__pyr_oid__`, `__pyr_session__` (the `_p_jar` analogue), `__pyr_tid__`. Tri-state Ghost→Clean→Dirty is unchanged but scoped: first-write hook registers in `s._dirty`, ghost activation hydrates via the session's snapshot connection (persistent pkg: "remains in the ghost state until the application attempts to access...at that point the object requests that its data manager load its state"). COMMIT: acquire `db._commit_lock` (SQLite is single-writer anyway, zero new restriction) → `SELECT oid,tid FROM objects WHERE oid IN (dirty)` → any tid != read_tid raises `Conflict(oid)` → else msgspec-encode the session's copies (traversal touches ONLY confined copies — race (a) is impossible by construction, not discipline), CAS-update blobs+tids under new_tid, feed commit-delta/watermark to FTS5/usearch/Arrow → reset dirty, re-arm hooks (race (b) gone: re-arming happens on the owning thread; no other thread can write those objects), refresh the read txn (end+begin = ZODB's `newTransaction`/`poll_invalidations` boundary). RETRY: `Conflict` subclasses TransientError; `@db.transactional` opens a FRESH session per attempt — "The most common way to deal with conflict errors is to catch them and retry transactions" (zodb.org). LRM DAEMON: never performs the two-op state-release+`__class__` swap cross-thread again. Request sessions need no demotion at all (they die in milliseconds; close drops the imap, GC frees everything). For the long-lived ambient session the daemon only *posts demotion requests* to a per-session queue; the owning thread drains it at safe points (commit boundaries, explicit `db.checkpoint()`), so the class-swap executes single-threaded — race (c) eliminated. Optional shared read cache `(oid, tid) -> msgpack bytes` (immutable values, one small lock) de-duplicates disk reads across sessions without sharing live objects.
options.implementation_cost: ~3–4 weeks solo, honest breakdown: Session class + per-session imap/dirty/read-tids (3–5 d; the buffer-until-commit storer and tri-state hook mostly re-scope from global to session); SQLite connection pool + per-session WAL read-txn lifecycle + checkpoint-starvation guard (2–3 d); TID CAS commit + Conflict + retry decorator (2–3 d); ambient session via ContextVar + DX sugar (1–2 d); cross-session detection in storer traversal (1 d); LRM rework to safe-point queues (2–3 d, mostly DELETING the dangerous cross-thread path); tests — conflict matrix, snapshot isolation, retry semantics, strawberry integration (4–6 d). Adds to v0.x: a `tid` column, a connection pool, session lifecycle, conflict machinery — each piece small; the real cost is conceptual commitment (sessions become THE core abstraction) plus ~1 week of doc to teach it. Counterweight: the alternative (EclipseStore-style shared graph) ships zero code but transfers an unbounded locking burden to users — EclipseStore needs an entire doc page (resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc) plus LockedExecutor/LockScope/StripeLockedExecutor helpers plus Spring @Read/@Write AOP to make it survivable, and still admits "the framework has nothing to lock around because the framework is not in the read/write path" (line 24).
options.steady_state_overhead: Measured on Python 3.14.0, Apple Silicon (uv run, msgspec): attribute access on a hydrated object = 0 added cost (plain slot access, no proxies). Ambient-session resolution: ContextVar.get() = 12 ns. First-write hook: 106 ns vs 65 ns plain slot setattr = +41 ns, once per object per transaction (then de-arms). Hydration: 0.34 µs/object (msgpack dict decode + slots-dataclass ctor, ~2.9 M obj/s); a typed-decoder fast path hits 0.20 µs (~5.0 M obj/s). So a GraphQL request touching 2,000 objects pays ~0.7 ms decode + SQLite point reads (~2–4 ms cold, near-zero with the shared (oid,tid)→bytes record cache) — that is the per-request tax vs. the shared graph's 0 ms. Memory: measured 271 B live per small 5-field slots dataclass; + imap entry + read_tid entry + string payloads ≈ the project's ~600 B/object envelope. Cost law: 600 B × objects-touched × concurrent sessions — e.g. 2 k-object requests × 32 concurrent ≈ 38 MB; a 50 k-object analytics traversal in a request session ≈ 30 MB *per request* (push those to Arrow mirrors instead). Commit adds one indexed SELECT over dirty OIDs (µs–low-ms) + CAS updates under a commit lock that changes nothing (SQLite is single-writer). ZODB precedent for pool sizing pressure: ConnectionPool warns at `pool_size=7` ("DB.open() has %s open connections with a pool_size of %s", ZODB/DB.py).
options.failure_modes: (1) CONFLICT HOTSPOTS — the classic ZODB failure: any popular object (root collection, a counter) makes concurrent commits abort each other; under load, retries livelock. ZODB needed `_p_resolveConflict` types and BTrees with bucket-level conflict resolution; pyrsistance would need analogous mergeable types (PCounter, append-only log) or must document "low write concurrency by design" (honest for the single-writer persona). (2) RETRY × SIDE EFFECTS — the canonical ZODB production bug: a transactional function sends an email/calls an API, conflicts, re-runs, sends twice. Only documentation defends against this. (3) OBJECT ESCAPE — user stashes a session object in a module global or closure; after close it raises SessionClosedError on access (loud, like SQLAlchemy's DetachedInstanceError) — better than silent staleness, but still a runtime surprise far from the cause. (4) CROSS-SESSION ASSIGNMENT detected only at commit traversal (late error, ZODB's InvalidObjectReference has the same distance-from-cause problem). (5) WRITE SKEW — snapshot isolation only: two sessions read overlapping state, write disjoint objects, jointly break an invariant; undetected unless the user opts into `s.assert_current(obj)` (ZODB's readCurrent analogue is equally opt-in). (6) Long-lived ambient session pins the SQLite WAL snapshot → unbounded WAL growth in a never-committing CLI process; needs boundary refresh + an idle-refresh nudge. (7) Identity surprises: `a is b` fails across sessions; id-keyed sets/caches spanning sessions silently misbehave. (8) Strawberry executes sync resolvers in a threadpool → two resolvers of ONE request can hydrate into one session concurrently; needs a per-session RLock around the load path (cheap) or async-only resolvers — concurrent WRITES within one request remain documented user error.
options.precedents: ZODB (25+ years, Plone/Zope): DB shared, Connection-per-thread from a pool (pool_size=7 default, ZODB/DB.py); per-Connection PickleCache identity map + ghosts ("an empty shell...when it's accessed, its state will be loaded automatically" — zodb.org writing-persistent-objects); `_p_jar` ownership with `InvalidObjectReference` on cross-connection use (ZODB/Connection.py `add()`); MVCC via timestamp/loadBefore ("timestamp-based commit protocol that provides snapshot isolation"), invalidations applied only at transaction boundaries (`newTransaction` → `storage.poll_invalidations()` → `cache.invalidate()`); ConflictError + retry ("for attempt in transaction.manager.attempts(): with attempt:" — zodb.org transactions guide); object states GHOST(-1)/UPTODATE(0)/CHANGED(1) with `_p_deactivate` (persistent.readthedocs.io/en/latest/using.html). Verdict: the model held up for decades of production CMS workloads; the pain was conflict-hot BTrees and retry-side-effect bugs, both manageable. SQLAlchemy Session: THE mainstream Python precedent — "a mutable, stateful object that represents a single database transaction...cannot be shared among concurrent threads or asyncio tasks", "Session per thread, AsyncSession per task", session-per-web-request as the documented norm (docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_basics.html). JPA/Hibernate: EntityManager-per-request is the same model in Java; ironically EclipseStore's own docs concede that the copy step is what gives ORMs mediated concurrency: "JPA hydrates entities into a session-scoped first-level cache...concurrency between threads is mostly mediated by the database" while for EclipseStore "the application owns the concurrency story" (resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc:18-24, plus the iron rule at :32 "Modifying the object graph and the corresponding store() call must happen under the same lock"). Datomic's db-as-value peer model endorses snapshot-per-reader from another angle.
options.integration_notes: TRI-STATE DIRTY TRACKING: survives intact, re-scoped — hook arms per session, first write registers in `s._dirty`, commit re-arms on the owning thread; the lost-update window (race b) disappears because no foreign thread can write a session's objects during the flip. LAZYREFERENCEMANAGER: the big winner — cross-thread demotion (race c) is deleted, not fixed. Request sessions need no demotion (close → drop strong imap → GC). The ambient session gets demotion as a safe-point operation on its own thread (daemon posts requests; session drains at commit/checkpoint), so the state-release + `__class__` swap pair runs single-threaded; alternatively weakref imap + LRU strong-ref release lets GC do the demotion. ASYNCIO FACADE: ContextVar-bound session propagates into `asyncio.to_thread` automatically (to_thread copies context), so a lazy load off-loaded to a thread still lands in the right session; rule "one session = one logical task" matches SQLAlchemy's AsyncSession contract verbatim. STRAWBERRY/GRAPHQL: textbook fit — open session in the context_getter/FastAPI dependency, queries read the snapshot, mutations commit at request end, Conflict → retry or 409; only caveat is strawberry's sync-resolver threadpool (per-session RLock on the hydration path, ~50 ns uncontended, or async-only resolvers). The workers=1 lock file stays (still single process, single writer). FREE-THREADED PYTHON: confinement is exactly what no-GIL wants — zero shared mutable graph, sessions scale reads across cores; only the (oid,tid)→bytes record cache is shared, and its values are immutable bytes under one lock. The commit-delta/watermark pipeline and Arrow mirrors are unchanged — Arrow's immutable snapshots are philosophically the same trick (snapshot views) applied to analytics.
options.migration_paths: THE ASYMMETRY IS THE HEADLINE. Reachable later FROM C: (1) the simple shared-graph DX — it is the degenerate case: `db.bind_ambient()` = one process-wide long-lived session; ships on day 1, not a migration at all; (2) actor/owner-thread confinement (pin a session to a writer thread, channel commands in) — trivial, sessions are already confined; (3) historical/time-travel read sessions `db.session(before=tid)` — nearly free once TIDs exist (ZODB's historical_pool precedent, ZODB/DB.py `historical_pool_size=3`); (4) client/server later (ZEO-style): the session/storage split is precisely the seam ZEO used — sessions talk to a remote storage without touching user code; (5) free-threaded parallel reads (above); (6) mergeable/conflict-resolving types can be added incrementally per class. FORECLOSED BY C (one-way doors closed): process-wide object identity — "one live instance per OID process-wide" and the single WeakValueDictionary registry are dead; any API that hands out persistent objects without a session context; the EclipseStore hybrid "share the live graph, lock around mutate+store" — you cannot half-share. Background threads can no longer mutate "the" graph ambiently; they must open sessions and may conflict where a locked shared graph would have serialized. THE REVERSE DOOR (the review's point d): starting with the shared map forecloses C — retrofitting per-session copies breaks every user reliance on global `is`-identity, id-keyed caches, and Lazy semantics, and the LRM/dirty machinery all assume one instance per OID. So C is the door-PRESERVING choice: shared→C is a breaking rewrite; C→shared is one method call. The 'default ambient session' genuinely gives both personas both worlds, at the cost of 12 ns per ambient lookup.
options.best_for: BEST: the FastAPI/strawberry integration persona (session-per-request is the textbook pattern users already know from SQLAlchemy — zero novel concepts for them); agent-memory apps with concurrent agent tasks (each task a session, conflicts retried); anyone needing read consistency during long traversals (snapshot isolation answers race (a) absolutely); the project's own future (free-threading, time-travel, client/server all stay open). The local-first/CLI/desktop persona is SERVED WELL ONLY via the ambient default session — with it, single-threaded code looks identical to the shared-graph design ('your objects ARE the database' survives as 'within your session — and you only have one'). WORST FOR: write-hot shared state (counters,队 popular collections) under real concurrency — conflict/retry livelock, the documented ZODB weakness; bulk analytics through live objects (600 B × 100 k objects per request session = 60 MB — use the Arrow mirrors instead, which is what they're for); DX purists who want literal global singleton objects and `is`-identity across the whole process — that pitch is genuinely weakened from 'objects are global singletons' to 'one instance per OID per session', the same softening every ORM accepted; and v0.x minimalism — this is the highest-concept-count option on the table, justified only because it is the one that answers all five review races by construction rather than by user discipline.

options.name: Option D — Owner-thread confinement (actor model): the store binds to one thread or event loop at open; all live-graph access is owner-only; foreign threads get command submission + immutable watermark snapshots
options.contract:
GUARANTEED on the owner (thread or loop bound at open): the full live-object API — root access, plain attribute reads/writes, Lazy.get()/ghost loads, EntityMap queries, store(), commit(). Commit serializes a graph that no other code can mutate mid-traversal (loop mode: serialization phase contains no awaits; thread mode: single-threaded), so persisted cross-object state always existed in memory — races (a) and (b) are closed by construction, not by locks. GUARANTEED from ANY thread: (1) store.submit(fn) -> concurrent.futures.Future, fn runs on the owner; (2) store.snapshot() -> immutable view pinned at watermark W (Arrow tables, frozen roaring bitmaps, frozen-DTO record decode) — safe under GIL and free-threaded builds. submit() results may not be live entities: returning one raises EntityEscapeError (cheap isinstance in future-resolution); return values/OIDs/frozen DTOs instead. UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR: touching a live entity, Lazy, or EntityMap from a non-owner thread. Production raises WrongThreadError at every API boundary (store/commit/Lazy.get/query/root); plain getattr on a leaked Clean object is NOT checked in production. debug=True keeps the __setattr__ hook permanently armed (checks owner on every write) and checks owner in ghost __getattribute__ — catches writes and lazy loads, documented as not catching plain reads of materialized state. API sketch:
```python
store = pyr.Store.open("app.pyr")          # owner = this thread (CLI / Qt main)
store = await pyr.Store.aopen("app.pyr")   # owner = this running event loop

# on owner: plain object programming, EclipseStore-style
store.root.orders.append(o); o.state = "paid"
store.commit()                  # sync mode
await store.commit()            # loop mode: chunked, loop-friendly

# from any other thread
snap = store.snapshot()                          # immutable, watermark-pinned
df  = snap.arrow(Order).to_pandas()              # zero-copy analytics
o2  = snap.get(oid)                              # frozen DTO (Realm-frozen analog)
fut = store.submit(lambda s: s.add_order(dto)); fut.result()
```
This is EclipseStore's own rule — "modifying the object graph and storing the changes must happen under the same lock" (resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc:30-33) — made structural instead of disciplinary: the 'lock' is the owner thread itself, and the runtime enforces it.
options.mechanics: BINDING + GUARD: open() records _owner_tid = threading.get_ident() (and _owner_loop if opened via aopen). Guard = one int compare; measured on this machine (CPython 3.14.0): 34-39 ns. Placed in production at API boundaries only; debug mode adds it to the (permanently-armed) __setattr__ hook and ghost __getattribute__. No epochs, no per-object locks, no copies — confinement IS the synchronization. SUBMISSION: loop mode = loop.call_soon_threadsafe bridging into a concurrent.futures.Future (measured: 13.0 µs blocking round trip, 4.5 µs/op pipelined ≈ 220k ops/s); coroutines via run_coroutine_threadsafe. Sync/inline mode (no loop): queue.SimpleQueue drained by store.pump() from the app's main loop (Qt timer), or opt-in ACTOR MODE where the store spawns its own owner thread running the pump and every API call from anywhere becomes a message. COMMIT (3 phases, closes races a+b): P1 on owner, zero awaits — drain the dirty list, traverse + swizzle + msgspec-encode into an immutable byte buffer, capture the EXACT object set; atomic w.r.t. the loop because nothing yields. P2 cooperative — SQLite blob write + fsync via await asyncio.to_thread (the worker thread touches only bytes, never the graph); plain blocking call in sync mode. P3 on owner, zero awaits — flip ONLY the P1-captured objects Dirty->Clean, re-arm their __setattr__ hooks, bump the watermark, enqueue sidecar deltas. Writes landing during P2 hit hooks re-armed in P1 and join the NEXT transaction's dirty set; since P3 flips only the captured set, an object re-dirtied during P2 stays Dirty — no lost update, no window, because arm/flip/write all execute on one thread. LAZYREFERENCEMANAGER WITHOUT RACES: it stops being a graph-touching thread. Loop mode: an asyncio.Task (sleep(1); demote batch ≤1 ms with no awaits inside a batch) — the two non-atomic demotion ops (state release + __class__ assignment) run interleaving-free on the loop, so race (c) vanishes by construction; contrast EclipseStore, whose lazy clearing is only safe because clear() keeps a WeakReference swizzle registry and delegates reclamation to the JVM GC (resources/store/docs/modules/storage/pages/loading-data/lazy-loading/clearing-lazy-references.adoc:8-14) — class-swap ghosts have no GC backstop, so confinement is the Python-shaped substitute. Sync mode hybrid: a daemon SENTINEL thread that never touches the graph — it reads lastTouched timestamps and RSS (torn-read-benign floats) and posts candidate OIDs; actual demotion piggybacks inside the next owner-side API call (store()/commit()/Lazy.get() check a flag, demote a ≤1 ms batch). SNAPSHOTS: commit P3 publishes watermark W; Arrow mirrors are append-only immutable batches + roaring tombstones, so snapshot() = capture (W, batch refs, frozen bitmap copies) in µs with no graph traversal; snap.get(oid) decodes the msgpack record at ≤W into a frozen, non-registered, non-Lazy DTO.
options.implementation_cost: ~2-3 weeks for a solo maintainer, and it is NET-NEGATIVE complexity for v0.x because it deletes planned work. Breakdown: owner binding + WrongThreadError guards at API boundaries: 1-2 days. submit()/pump()/actor mode incl. self-deadlock guard (submit from owner runs inline or raises): 3-5 days with tests. Refactor buffer-until-commit storer into the 3-phase commit (most effort is tests for re-dirty-during-P2 semantics): 3-5 days. LazyReferenceManager conversion to loop-task + sentinel hybrid: 2-4 days — this REMOVES the per-object demotion lock / epoch scheme the adversarial review demanded (STRESS-TEST.md remediation 1: 'specify the LazyReferenceManager's demotion protocol (per-object lock or epoch scheme)'), which would have been 4-8 weeks of permanent lock-ordering complexity under any shared-graph option. Debug-mode armed hooks: 1-2 days. Threading guide + asyncio critical-section docs: 2-3 days. What it ADDS to v0.x: WrongThreadError, submit/pump/actor plumbing, snapshot() facade over the already-planned Arrow mirror, and a hard requirement that the watermark/snapshot pipeline ships in v0.1 rather than v0.2 (frozen reads are the pressure valve that makes confinement livable — Realm's lesson).
options.steady_state_overhead: Measured on the target interpreter (CPython 3.14.0, this machine). Plain attribute access on owner: +0 ns in production — no guard on getattr/setattr beyond the existing tri-state hook design (7.4 ns slots read unchanged; the design's zero-steady-state-overhead claim survives intact). First-write: the tri-state __setattr__ hook cost is unchanged in production; debug mode keeps the hook armed permanently (debug-only ~100-200 ns/write). Lazy.get(): guard adds +31 ns on the warm path (22.4 -> 53.2 ns measured); a guard-on-cold-path-only option restores 0 ns warm while still catching the dangerous case (foreign-thread loads). store()/commit() guard: 34-39 ns — noise against ms-scale commits. Commit latency: P1 loop stall ≈ 2-3 ms per 10k dirty objects (DESIGN.md figures: 18-26 ns/obj traversal + 93 ns/obj msgspec encode, plus swizzling ≈ 150-300 ns/dirty obj); 100k dirty ≈ 20-30 ms stall — document p99, offer incremental commit later; P2 (I/O, the slow part) is off-loop. Cross-thread command: 13 µs blocking round trip, 4.5 µs/op pipelined (~220k ops/s ceiling for foreign-thread writes). snapshot(): µs-scale (pointer copies + frozen bitmap copy), no traversal, no pause.
options.failure_modes: (1) Leaked live entity: a closure/global/submit-result smuggles an entity to a foreign thread; plain getattr there is unguarded in production — manifests as stale reads under the GIL, upgrades to torn reads/AttributeError crashes under free-threading if it races demotion or commit P3. Mitigations: EntityEscapeError on submit results, debug mode, docs; residual risk is real and is THE failure mode of this model. (2) Cross-await logical races (asyncio): confinement gives memory safety and commit consistency, NOT request isolation — two interleaved FastAPI requests mutating the same objects across await points produce a commit that mixes both requests' partial work; correct code keeps mutate+commit-P1 in one await-free section (EclipseStore's same-lock rule, restated for asyncio: 'a critical section is the code between awaits'); ship `async with store.transaction()` (an asyncio.Lock) for mutating handlers. Manifests as business-invariant violations that look exactly like race (a) but are application bugs, not engine bugs — the docs must make this distinction loudly or users will file engine bugs. (3) Loop stalls: big commit P1 or a user's sync work on the loop — latency spikes, never corruption; 100k-dirty commits stall ~20-30 ms. (4) FastAPI sync `def` endpoints / dependencies run in anyio's threadpool -> first store touch raises WrongThreadError — fail-fast and safe, but a guaranteed support-ticket generator; the integration should detect and auto-submit or emit an actionable message ('declare this endpoint async or use store.submit'). (5) Self-deadlock: owner calling fut.result() on its own submission — guard runs it inline or raises. (6) Idle sync apps never demote (no loop, no API calls to piggyback on) -> RSS stays high until the next store call or explicit store.maintenance(); the sentinel thread can only nag, not act. (7) Qt: a long commit on the bound main thread freezes the UI — use actor mode, or pump-based commit. (8) Throughput ceiling: all writes funnel through one thread (~220k submitted ops/s; in-owner writes are unbounded) — not a bug, but the wall a wrong-fit user hits.
options.precedents: Realm / MongoDB Atlas Device SDK — the closest precedent: live objects and Realm instances are thread-confined (cross-thread access throws 'Realm accessed from incorrect thread' / IncorrectThreadException), MVCC underneath, per-thread versions advanced on run-loop ticks; frozen objects (realm-java 7.0 / realm-cocoa 5.0, 2020) and ThreadSafeReference were added AFTER years of confinement exceptions being the #1 developer complaint. Outcome: dominant mobile object DB; lesson encoded in this design: ship the frozen/snapshot read path (Arrow snapshots, snap.get) in v0.1, not as a retrofit. Python sqlite3 — check_same_thread=True default for 20+ years ('SQLite objects created in a thread can only be used in that same thread'); proof the Python ecosystem accepts confinement-by-default in the stdlib. DuckDB Python — docs require one thread per connection/cursor (per-thread .cursor() pattern). JavaScript/Node — the largest-scale proof: one loop owns all objects, workers get structured-clone copies; an entire industry ships on owner-loop + message passing + immutable transfer. Erlang ETS — every table has exactly one owner process; 'protected' mode = owner writes, all read (precisely owner + snapshots); 30 years battle-tested. Redis — single-threaded command execution fed by client queues; later I/O threads deliberately kept data access single-threaded. Datomic — single-writer transactor + immutable snapshot reads on every peer: commands+snapshots at datacenter scale. ZODB — 'databases are thread-safe, connections are not; one connection per thread': Python-persistence orthodoxy is already session confinement; pyrsistance's single identity map means there is exactly one 'connection', hence one owner. EclipseStore itself — its docs put the burden on the app, its simplest sanctioned strategy is coarse XThreads.executeSynchronized, i.e. effectively single-threaded ('This guarantees correctness at the cost of throughput', resources/store/docs/modules/intro/pages/concurrent-access.adoc:144-157), and its Storer and Serializer are ALREADY documented as thread-confined ('Serializer instances must be confined to a single thread'; 'A Storer is a per-thread unit of work... must not be shared across threads', concurrent-access.adoc:117-125). Option D does not diverge from EclipseStore — it takes EclipseStore's first strategy and its confined internals and makes them the enforced contract. Locking helpers reference: resources/store/docs/modules/misc/pages/locking/index.adoc.
options.integration_notes: TRI-STATE DIRTY TRACKING: becomes owner-only code — the self-removing __setattr__ hook needs no atomicity defense; commit's capture-set flip (P1/P3) closes race (b) exactly because arm, write, and flip can never interleave; debug mode is literally 'the hook stops self-removing'. The hook is also the natural place for the debug ownership assert. LAZYREFERENCEMANAGER: demotion moves on-owner (loop task in async mode, sentinel+piggyback in sync mode); race (c) — the strongest finding of the adversarial review, present even in 'single-threaded' apps — vanishes by construction, AND the class-swap ghost optimization becomes safe to ship at all, since __class__ assignment now never races a reader; the per-object lock/epoch protocol demanded by STRESS-TEST.md remediation 1 is simply deleted. ASYNCIO FACADE: stops being a facade — loop mode makes the store loop-native; race (e) ('async facade routes lazy loads through threads') disappears because lazy loads execute on the loop (via the DataLoader/default_resolver awaitable path for batching, or a sub-ms blocking record read) and to_thread only ever sees serialized bytes. STRAWBERRY/GRAPHQL: with workers=1 enforced, uvicorn runs exactly one loop -> every async resolver executes on the owner automatically — the flagship integration becomes trivially memory-safe with zero per-resolver ceremony. Verified in the cloned strawberry source: strawberry core never offloads sync resolvers to a threadpool (only the Django channels handler wraps with database_sync_to_async — resources/strawberry/strawberry/channels/handlers/http_handler.py:391-394); graphql-core runs sync resolvers inline on the loop, so they are owner-safe but loop-blocking — fine for identity-map hits, document for cold loads. Remaining GraphQL hazards: cross-await logical interleaving between concurrent requests (provide store.transaction() asyncio.Lock + the 'no awaits between mutate and commit' rule) and FastAPI sync-def endpoints in the anyio threadpool (WrongThreadError fail-fast; integration emits an actionable fix). Per-request DataLoaders (cache=False, primed from the identity map) are unchanged and batch sibling ghost loads into one storage read. FREE-THREADED PYTHON: the contract is unchanged and the future-proofing is REAL for the core — confinement never relied on GIL serialization (unlike the shared-graph alternative, where __class__-swap demotion racing getattr was 'papered over by the GIL' per the stress test), and the cross-thread surface is exclusively thread-safe primitives (queues, Futures, call_soon_threadsafe) plus immutable Arrow buffers/frozen bitmaps. Two honest caveats: leaked-entity getattr upgrades from stale-read to torn-read on 3.14t, raising the value of debug mode and EntityEscapeError; and the genuine 3.14t upside lands on the SNAPSHOT side — multiple threads crunching Arrow snapshots truly in parallel while the owner keeps serving, sidestepping the measured 2.5x free-threaded pointer-chasing penalty on the live graph (DESIGN.md).
options.migration_paths: REACHABLE LATER (doors held open): (1) Realm-style MVCC with per-thread read views — confinement is the substrate Realm itself built MVCC on; frozen-record snapshots are step one of that path. (2) Multiple stores = multiple owners (store-per-tenant / store-per-agent, ETS-style) with cross-store commands — needs no contract change. (3) Actor mode -> out-of-process server: once every foreign interaction is already a serialized command, ZEO-style client-server is a transport swap, not a model change. (4) A stop-the-world write-barrier or STM mode can be ADDED behind the same gates, since every entry point is already guarded. (5) Free-threaded parallel snapshot analytics widens without touching the contract. (6) Internals freedom: because foreign threads can never observe live objects, identity-map internals, ghost mechanics, and demotion strategy can all be swapped without user-visible breakage. FORECLOSED (one-way doors): ZODB-style per-session live-object copies stay foreclosed — but by the already-decided SINGLE shared identity map (race-review point d), not by Option D; Option D is the contract that makes one identity map SOUND, and snapshot reads deliver most of what per-session copies buy (concurrent consistent readers) without per-session heap. Genuinely closed by D itself: a future 'shared graph + fine-grained RW-locks, mutate from any thread' model — once users build on owner-only semantics and on submit()/snapshot(), re-opening cross-thread live access is a breaking contract change; likewise any transparent-proxy design where Lazy.get() is callable from arbitrary threads. Verdict: D converts the identity-map one-way door from a liability into the load-bearing wall, and keeps more future doors open than any locking scheme, because contracts about who MAY touch the graph can be relaxed later — memory-corruption bugs shipped under a looser contract cannot.
options.best_for: BEST: exactly the declared persona. Local-first/CLI/desktop apps are naturally single-threaded — they pay 0 ns and gain a corruption-free guarantee plus safe background analytics via snapshots; Qt apps bind to the main thread (or actor mode) with worker threads on snapshots/commands; agent-memory loops are single-loop by nature; and the flagship FastAPI/strawberry workers=1 deployment becomes the showcase — every async resolver lands on the owner for free, making 'thread-safe by construction on asyncio' a launch differentiator no shared-graph design can claim. The snapshot path serves the pandas/Arrow analytics persona from any thread at zero risk. WORST: multi-threaded write-heavy workloads — thread-pool ETL mutating entities in parallel, classic sync-handler web tiers, multi-loop/multi-worker servers — all writes funnel through one owner (13 µs round trip, ~220k submitted ops/s ceiling) and the model offers them nothing but a queue; also casual Jupyter/threading users who will meet WrongThreadError and must learn submit()/snapshot(). Those workloads should batch through commands, read through Arrow, or use a different database — and the contract lets the docs say so honestly.
