judgment.recommendation: Option D — owner-thread/loop confinement — with two riders borrowed from B and C, and that hybrid is principled, not a fudge, because the layers are orthogonal: confinement answers WHO may touch the graph (memory safety, closes races a/b/c/e by construction), while B's scope concept answers WHEN logical work is atomic — so ship `async with store.transaction()` (an asyncio.Lock) in the FastAPI/strawberry integration by default to handle cross-await interleaving, and ship snapshot()/Arrow watermark reads in v0.1 (Realm's frozen-objects lesson: the pressure valve cannot be a retrofit). The reverse hybrid ("D contract with B's barrier internally") IS a fudge: under owner-only semantics there are no concurrent mutators for a barrier to exclude, so it would be dead code that only softens UB and muddies the teaching contract. D wins on the persona's own terms: the local-first/CLI/agent 90% write the exact naive hello-world (zero ceremony, 0 ns added) and get corruption-freedom enforced rather than documented — the precedent record (MicroStream #394/#453/#676 despite java.util.concurrent literacy, then the 2024 Locking-API capitulation) proves Option A's discipline-based contract fails in the field, and 2026 Python culture is one notch worse. The flagship asyncio integration becomes trivially owner-safe (workers=1 ⇒ one loop ⇒ every async resolver runs on the owner), deleting race (e) instead of locking around it. It is the only option that is net-negative complexity for a solo maintainer (deletes the demotion lock/epoch protocol STRESS-TEST.md:105 demands) and the only one that rescues the class-swap ghost optimization on both GIL and free-threaded builds (demotion runs on-owner, so __class__ assignment never races a reader). Doors it deliberately closes: cross-thread access to live entities (mutate-from-any-thread shared graph can never be re-opened once users build on submit()/snapshot()), transparent any-thread Lazy.get(), and — knowingly cemented, though already closed by the decided single WeakValueDictionary — ZODB-style writable per-session copies; D converts that liability into the wall the whole design stands on, while C-style READ-ONLY sessions (snapshot, historical-before-watermark) remain additive on the existing commit-delta/watermark pipeline.
judgment.decide_now: (1) The contract itself = D, including binding semantics (owner = opening thread, or loop via aopen) — it shapes every public entry point, the error taxonomy (WrongThreadError, EntityEscapeError), hook semantics, daemon architecture, and commit pipeline; relaxing confinement later is additive, but retrofitting it after users share live objects across threads is a breaking change, and STRESS-TEST.md:105 explicitly requires the contract before v0.1 code. (2) Three-phase commit ordering (P1 on-owner await-free capture+encode; P2 off-loop I/O on bytes only; P3 on-owner flip-only-the-captured-set + re-arm + watermark bump) — this closes race (b) and the buffer-until-commit storer must be built in this shape from its first version; re-dirty-during-P2 semantics are the core test surface. (3) The daemon principle: LazyReferenceManager NEVER touches the live graph from a foreign thread (loop-task in async mode; sentinel-posts + owner-side piggyback in sync mode) — closes race (c), which bites even single-threaded apps, and note this principle is required under EVERY option on the table, so it is contract-independent and must be specified now. (4) Promote the watermark/snapshot pipeline (Arrow batches + frozen bitmaps + frozen-DTO snap.get) from v0.2 to v0.1 — D is only livable with the frozen read path; this is a roadmap re-sequencing decision. (5) Record an ADR affirming the one-shared-identity-map door knowingly: writable per-session copies are foreclosed (they already were, by the decided WeakValueDictionary registry); deciding this explicitly now prevents relitigating it at v0.5. (6) The asyncio doctrine in the contract doc from day one: "a critical section is the code between awaits; mutate + commit-P1 with no await between, or use store.transaction()" — EclipseStore's same-lock rule (concurrent-access.adoc core principle) restated for the event loop.
judgment.deferrable: All deferrable WITHOUT rework because every entry point is already guarded and foreign interaction is already serialized: actor mode (store spawns its own owner thread; pure facade over submit()); Realm-style MVCC per-thread read views (confinement is the substrate Realm built MVCC on; frozen snapshots are step one); read-only historical sessions `db.session(before=watermark)` (additive on the existing commit-delta/TID pipeline — C's only surviving piece, reachable later); incremental/chunked commit to shrink the P1 stall beyond ~100k dirty objects; C-extension rewrite of the 34-39 ns guards and any RWLock-class primitives; the FastAPI sync-def auto-submit shim polish (v0.1 needs only the actionable WrongThreadError message); out-of-process ZEO-style actor server (transport swap once everything is commands); multi-store-per-tenant (ETS-style, no contract change); free-threaded cp314t wheels and parallel snapshot analytics; debug-mode tooling depth (permanently-armed hooks + ghost __getattribute__ owner checks ship v0.1 minimal, leak-detection heuristics later); mergeable/CRDT-ish types — only relevant if the C door ever reopens, i.e. probably never.
judgment.dissent: The strongest argument against D favors Option C, and it is a timing argument: pre-v0.1 with zero users is the ONLY moment the shared-identity-map one-way door can be reversed for free, and D doesn't just inherit that closure — it builds the entire contract on top of it, making reversal maximally expensive forever. The secondary persona is exactly where C is mainstream Python orthodoxy (session-per-request is SQLAlchemy's documented contract verbatim, ZODB's 25-year deployment model, JPA's EntityManager pattern — users arrive already knowing it), and D conspicuously does NOT give those users request isolation: two interleaved FastAPI requests mutating across await points still produce commingled commits, so D users must learn store.transaction() anyway — meaning D's zero-ceremony advantage over C-with-ambient-session shrinks to ~nothing for the integration persona, while C answers all five review races PLUS request isolation PLUS write-conflict detection by construction, and keeps the most future doors open (time-travel, client/server via the session/storage seam, true free-threaded parallel reads). If agent-memory apps trend multi-tenant/server-ward over the 24-month horizon — plausible for agent platforms — choosing D now means the v2 rewrite C was designed to avoid, whereas choosing C now costs only ~1-2 extra weeks and 12 ns per ambient lookup. The counter that still carries the decision: a solo maintainer shipping v0.1 in weeks cannot afford C's concept count, conflict/retry failure classes, and ~1 week of teaching docs to serve a primary persona that is single-threaded by nature — and C's ambient-session mode is, operationally, D with extra machinery underneath.
